sentences.text: Moisei, Moisei.
Yes.
Well, the-- it started in 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1944 [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] came an order [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the town that we shall get ready, and pack up.
And we going to go into the next town, all together, all Jewish people.
And anybody will obey, they have a list from every kid, and everyone in the family.
Anybody will be missed, the parents will be shot.
I beg your pardon?
There was-- before this happened, and overnight, about two weeks before, they took away from the town-- from the village rather-- they took away the rabbi, and the leadership from the village.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My-- my grandfather was the president of the town many years before.
They didn't take him.
They took the president of the town, what was the president in 1944.
And they took a couple of intellectuals, about half a dozen people, overnight, just took them out from their houses.
And they never-- we never heard-- never knew anything.
Up to this, never knew what happened.
And he went every two, three hours, [INAUDIBLE] drummed.
And the people came out.
And he read out from the paper, about the law, and what we would do, and what going to be done.
And came out the day before, we shall get ready.
And tomorrow morning, there's going to be wagons.
And as much as we can carry, everybody can take along covers, and so on, only brought as much as a person can carry, and put on the wagon.
And we'll go into next town.
Yes, yes, we had-- people were taken away from the village to-- they called it [GERMAN], so to work on to the front for the Nazis, for the Hungarians.
People in 28, from 25 on, 21 years and up till 35, they took these men, Jewish people, to a labor camp, labor.
And one-- two of them ran away and came back.
And he came back to town a year or six months before we were evacuated.
And a matter of fact, he was a very religious man.
And he took a [NON-ENGLISH] and a [NON-ENGLISH].
And he came back.
And he said that I saw with my-- saw with my eyes, they took gasoline, and put on barracks, and put on fire people-- they locked them in and burned them, Jewish people.
And when they came back, it was so far-fetched that nobody-- nobody could believe it.
And they said, he's off his [NON-ENGLISH].
And the other guy was a-- he was a businessman.
The other guy was a butcher.
And he really, when he came back, he made himself crazy.
And he told his stories.
And he became crazy.
They sent him back, or somehow he escaped.
I don't know exactly.
Now, we could-- we heard about it.
We couldn't-- we couldn't believe it.
And we could not-- just, I mean, that human beings will do a thing like this.
No, no.
No, no, not much.
Well, we-- next morning, we were talking, mother and father thought maybe the bigger boys should run away, and go away to the mountains.
We had mountains.
And here, the law is if, you know, that anybody, parents will be shot.
As many kids are going to miss-- anybody missed from the family-- we were 11, so many people are going to killed, starting with the parents.
So there is what-- so very, very close family.
And we packed up.
And we went to next town.
The whole family?
We took covers.
We had some silver, [NON-ENGLISH], what we used the candles for Shabbas, dishes, and only what we were able to carry, and whatever food we had available.
Books, no, very little, very little.
My father took some [NON-ENGLISH], a couple of-- took the [NON-ENGLISH].
We went to next town in [NON-ENGLISH].
And there, matter of fact, we stayed in the same area where my-- in the same house.
I had an uncle there.
And that whole area, half of that town became the ghetto.
And all the towns and villages, all the Jews were brought in there.
And we stayed together in the town, uncle and aunt with their kids.
What I remember is, well, four to six weeks.
[NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
And the town, we were talking for escaping, escaping.
And my brother-- older brothers than me, Mende.
He was two years older than me.
And he was tall, strong guy.
And he and the other-- he had a partner in the lumber business.

And he and other boys in that age, four of them, somehow at night made themselves papers, somewhere in the ghetto, and escaped, and ran out of the ghetto, dressed in the-- you know, the gentiles were wearing a different costume of uniform-- white clothes, instead of regular material clothes.
They made their own clothes.
And they left.
And I never heard from them.
I never from those four guys again, till after the war.
And I-- and so they caught them while-- in the-- in the town on the mountains.
And they brought them in to the concentration camps.
And they-- all of them disappeared.
They're not alive.
In that town, they had transports.
The train, they gave orders that [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] one transport, they loaded up to Auschwitz.
We didn't know in that time where, what.
No, they gave an order, the town there, we had a Jewish community.
And they said a certain amount, the names from the ghetto.
And they went with the first transport they had, loaded up the train.
I don't know-- remember-- many many wagons, cattle wagons.
Ad women and children, grandparents, and so on.
We went on the second transport.
They had-- from [NON-ENGLISH], from this ghetto, they had three transports of train, loaded up the trains.
And we went into the train.
I went in my father and mother, my two grandfathers, and-- and an uncle, two uncles, and two aunts, children, and other people, packed in like sardines.
And it was very, very terrible, terrible.
I don't-- I don't know.
We were very, very packed, crowded.
And I remember one thing in the wagon.
He went ahead in a corner of the train, and with knives-- we didn't have tools-- he made a hole in the-- in the wagon, and put up a piece of curtain.
And anybody had to go, went-- they went there.
And the smell still was unbearable in the-- from the kids, and the things.
I remember once-- once they opened the wagons, and a free-- free area, where they gave us water.
You know, we run over for water, and they gave us water.
And it was spring.
The trees were blooming.
The grass was green in the area where the train stopped.
And we went for-- for 10, 15 minutes.
And we went out, and got water.
And we went back into the wagons.
No, cattle car-- a cattle car, and the doors were going in, and out.
And you just lock it from outside.
And the only thing what you had in that wagon, you have two small windows on top-- one on one end from the wagon, and one on the other end from the wagon.
I beg your pardon/
Yeah.
If I looked-- if I looked back and I look, I couldn't see the end of it.
I beg your pardon?
Yes.
Yes, yes.
I would-- five, six days, almost a week, five, six days.
Day and night.
I might remember day and night.
I remember the mornings.
And they used to put on [INAUDIBLE].
[SOBBING] I Among my grandfather [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] had [INAUDIBLE] all day.
[SOBBING]
No.
No, it's all right.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] OK, go.
No, we have the people on the wagon.
I saw the people-- the other people from the town, from my town.
No, we stopped the car-- empty field, empty fields.
[INAUDIBLE] prepared there, empty field.
I mean, you couldn't hide.
You could see miles and miles, empty fields, greenery, and small little trees.
April.
Food, whatever we had, and had taken along, what we had in the bags.
We had bread.
We had-- we had food.
We had a little bit of food, we had.
No, we didn't have.
We shared.
Everybody had-- we shared.
And we made-- everybody--
Rationed it, yeah.
The German officers, the German-- the Nazis, the soldiers.
They were with their machine guns outside, and all on the line, lined up, every wagon [INAUDIBLE] two, three machine guns.
Stood there, and we just ordered-- they had their own people order what we should do, get out, and get water, and get right away back in.
No.
[GERMAN] with sticks or something, nothing out of line.
We arrived in Auschwitz.
It was still in April.
We arrived in middle of the night, or in the evening.
But they didn't open the wagons till daylight in the morning, in the morning.
In the train in Auschwitz-- we didn't know where Auschwitz, not Auschwitz.
We didn't know what it is, or where we are.
Now, we know.
Now, we talk about it.
We didn't know.
In the morning--
They opened the wagons.
We jump-- we stayed up.
And everybody tried to look.
We couldn't see the window to look where-- where we are, or what it is.
I remember when they opened the wagons, my mother, she rest in peace, asked a Polish young man who spoke Yiddish, working on the platform there.
And one of them, number one, I remember got hurt from a capo.
He was with stripes.
And now I know, it was a capo in a striped uniform.
He had a striped uniform.
That he had a band.
And he hit them over the head.
And he said [GERMAN], watch one thing.
They shouldn't hit you on the head.
Be [INAUDIBLE], be fast.
They shouldn't hit you on the head.
Yeah, there were soldiers-- the German soldiers, they had machine guns, or guns, machine guns, all over.
And-- and they told us where they will lead us.
They told us what to do.
Women, we should leave all the packages.
And women that side, and the men on this side, and they separated us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the packages were left, something you could carry it in the hand, or some-- I don't remember exactly carrying anything.
And I remember one thing came in front of-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he's called.
Now, I know he was Mengele, his name.
And there was big, two dogs, to offer-- to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gestapo, to Germans with the machine guns.
He was in the middle with white gloves.
Their boots were shining, like all mighty, who knows what.
And he just-- he came in front of-- on the line, come to our line.
We were five, six in the line, mostly families.
We were staying together.
And the women were right next to us, not far in the line too.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So I remember the mother send over to the father, one of my brothers, little brothers, six, seven years old.
There was a brother and sister, twins, and to ask father if they should give out the twins.
They asking, twins shall come out.
And the [INAUDIBLE] went around, twins should go out.
And he sneaked it over before we got in the front.
And father said, no, positively not.
It was a boy and a girl.
To give-- they were asking for twins, any twins between the-- between the people, to give them out, to come out.
And my mother said-- asked father what she shall do.
And he said, no.
This is what I remember.
And then we walked further in the front, in front of Mengele.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the father, my father had my two grandfathers [INAUDIBLE], my mother's father and his father.
And he was-- my father was a tall man, a strong man.
He could have survived, could of, maybe yes, maybe.
I don't know.
But-- and he says-- he was holding the-- holding the two grandparents of my-- Mengele said to him-- me and my brother.
I had an older brother and me.
We go to to right, or the left, whatever, to the side.
And my father, he made also he should go.
And he said, what he should do with the-- then he said, no, go with them.
All three, and that's the way he went to the-- the extermination, what I understand today.
I didn't know to--
About 43, 44.
Well, there was nobody else in there.
I was told.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wouldn't know-- I-- after the concentration camp, we talked about him.
I saw pictures.
I don't think I can recognize him today.
Today, [INAUDIBLE] maybe.
They went-- went also to the-- with the children.
Children, right.
I had one-- one sister went also to work-- one-- two years or a year and a half younger than me.
She didn't-- she worked in Auschwitz in the camps there.
She didn't survive.
And I had an older brother to me, also in Auschwitz, two years older.
He didn't survive.
No, the labor camp.
I was in Auschwitz with him.
And they-- in Auschwitz, they-- I was in Auschwitz a short time, only two, three days.
And the one-- I didn't do anything there.
Just in the morning, appellplatz, cleaned the streets, swept two or three days.
And then they-- one morning, they kept us on the appellplatz, counting, counting.
We were thousands of people there.
And one day, they counted.
I was arm to arm with my brother.
And one day, they counted.
And my brother was next to my right and.
And they counted me up.
I had to go to bed.
And my brother remained there.
And that's all I ever heard and ever saw.
Yeah.
In Auschwitz, too.
When we went in-- I got a striped uniform, and pants, [NON-ENGLISH], a hat, and a jacket, and a t-shirt, or whatever, and an underwear or something.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that's about-- with a number-- a number with a yellow--
A yellow star right on the corner from the number.
And the number I remember 3642.
Still remember that number.
I'll never forget.
